Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I made it work. I'd usually dedicate about 24 hours a week to studying, mostly in long blocks when I could find the time between work and other responsibilities. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time making notes and rewriting concepts in my own words. The routine wasn't glamorous, but I had to be strategic because I don't have a lot of resources to fall back on.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I couldn't afford to be rigid about timing because sometimes work shifts changed or unexpected things came up. When deadlines approached, I'd cram in more hours wherever I could find them. It wasn't ideal, but flexibility was the only way I could survive this course.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or assignments posted that prompted me to log in. I logged in pretty frequently—about 41 times throughout the course—because I was always worried I'd miss something important. The anxiety of falling behind kept me coming back to check, even when I didn't have time to actually do much work. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in too, they made logging in feel slightly less daunting.
Q4: Yeah, eventually logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone first thing. I think it happened because I was so scared of missing deadlines or important updates. The fear kind of trained me into the habit. Once the gamified elements were introduced, there was also this small pull to see my progress, which helped make it feel more routine rather than just stressful.
Q5: The video content was actually really valuable to me—I watched over 147 minutes worth because hearing concepts explained helped them stick. The discussion forums were also useful; I posted 15 times and interacted with peers about 25 times because sometimes talking through ideas with others clarified things I couldn't understand alone. Reading materials that I could download and annotate were probably the most practical for my learning style though.
Q6: I often ignored or delayed the optional supplementary readings because I simply didn't have time. When you're working with limited resources and high stress, you have to prioritize ruthlessly. Some of the interactive elements felt like they took too much time for what they offered. I'd skim them quickly or skip them entirely if they weren't directly tied to assignments or exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me actually understand a difficult concept. I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt like busywork. The gamified tasks helped because they gave me a sense of progress and small wins, which I desperately needed when everything felt overwhelming. If something felt like it would move the needle on my grade, I was all in.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones had this weight of necessity—I had to do them, no matter how anxious or exhausted I was. Optional stuff, even when interesting, often got pushed aside because my bandwidth was so limited. There was always this calculation in my head about whether the time investment would pay off.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study them on my phone during commutes or breaks at work. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing helps me process information. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen, just me and my notes, trying to make sense of everything.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd print out lecture notes and study them at the library or at home without internet. I'd quiz myself using flashcards I made by hand. Some of my best study sessions were completely offline, going over my notes again and again until concepts clicked.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a resource once, download it, and then that PDF would live on my phone or laptop for weeks. I'd return to it multiple times offline, highlighting and annotating as I went. The LMS would only show that single access, but that material might have been reviewed five or six times on my own.
Q12: There were definitely periods when engagement became really hard. When my stress peaked and anxiety was high, sometimes I'd feel paralyzed and scared to even look at the coursework. Financial pressures and work demands would pile up, and suddenly the course felt like one more thing crushing me. Those were the moments when my attendance and assignment completion really suffered.
Q13: Massively. Fatigue from working to support myself meant some nights I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away from studying. When I was confused about material, the anxiety would spike and I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely for a day or two just to cope. It was a constant juggling act.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings and my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills or just too exhausted to focus. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt hollow, like going through the motions without any real absorption happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I'd like to admit, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel impossible sometimes—the fear of not doing well enough would freeze me up. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that tomorrow kept getting pushed back. It's a terrible cycle when you're scared and overwhelmed.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They forced me into action when nothing else could. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find reserves of energy I didn't know I had. But they also spiked my anxiety to unbearable levels. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before due dates because that's when the fear of failing finally outweighed the fear of trying.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to engage more—it felt like proof that I could actually do this. But critical feedback sometimes sent me spiraling, making me avoid the course for a bit while I processed it. I tried to use feedback constructively, but with high anxiety, it wasn't always easy to receive.
Q18: The structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a roadmap. But the gamified elements were what really helped me stay engaged despite everything. They broke things into smaller, less intimidating pieces. Without that structure, I think I would have felt even more lost and scared navigating everything on my own.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me small wins and a sense of progress that my anxious brain desperately needed. What made it harder was when expectations weren't clear or when there was too much material released at once. With limited resources and time, ambiguity was my enemy. Clear, chunked content was my friend.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard to engage but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. Or maybe they're dealing with stuff outside the course that prevents them from completing assignments. High activity might show effort and intention, while low submissions might show barriers that aren't visible—anxiety, perfectionism, life circumstances.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My attendance was only 7% and my assignment completion was low, but I still managed a Merit grade because I studied intensively offline. Someone looking at just my LMS activity might think I wasn't trying, but I was working incredibly hard—just not always in ways the system could track. Exam performance can sometimes tell a different story than daily activity.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you access, how often you engage with certain materials. But they miss the quality and depth of that engagement entirely. They can't see the hours I spent with printed notes, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the real understanding I built away from the screen. They capture behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's showing up mentally even when you're scared or exhausted. For me, engagement meant fighting through anxiety to participate in discussions, watching videos multiple times until concepts made sense, and refusing to give up even when everything felt impossible. It's an internal commitment that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how hard I fought to be here. It couldn't capture the fear I felt before every login or the relief when I finally understood something. It missed all my offline studying, my handwritten notes, the peer study sessions over coffee. Most importantly, it couldn't show my resilience—how I kept going despite limited resources, high stress, and constant anxiety. That's the real story of my engagement.
